nodes.x: Then Job answered and said,
But Job answered and said,
And Job answered and said,
Then Job answered the Lord, and said,
¶ Then Job answered the Lord, and said,
Moreover the Lord answered Job, and said,
I know it is so of a truth: but how should man be just with God?
How then can man be justified with God? or how can he be clean that is born of a woman?
But that no man is justified by the law in the sight of God, it is evident: for, The just shall live by faith.
Let us he pure and keep the commandments of God, and let the world say and do what they please. These are my feelings all the time.
God help us to do right and preserve our purity, keep his laws and lead us in the paths of life, that while we live upon the earth we may operate with him in the salvation of
Wherefore, let every man beware lest he do that which is not in truth and righteousness before me.
If he will contend with him, he cannot answer him one of a thousand.
Shall he that contendeth with the Almighty instruct him? he that reproveth God, let him answer it.
And they could not answer him again to these things.

And cast him into the bottomless pit, and shut him up, and set a seal upon him, that he should deceive the nations no more, till the thousand years should be fulfilled: and after that he must be loosed a little season.
Or what king, going to make war against another king, sitteth not down first, and consulteth whether he be able with ten thousand to meet him that cometh against him with twenty thousand?
Will ye accept his person? will ye contend for God?
He is wise in heart, and mighty in strength: who hath hardened himself against him, and hath prospered?
And his name through faith in his name hath made this man strong, whom ye see and know: yea, the faith which is by him hath given him this perfect soundness in the presence of you all.
Nevertheless he that standeth steadfast in his heart, having no necessity, but hath power over his own will, and hath so decreed in his heart that he will keep his virgin, doeth well.
Lo, this is the man that made not God his strength; but trusted in the abundance of his riches, and strengthened himself in his wickedness.
He hath dispersed, he hath given to the poor; his righteousness endureth for ever; his horn shall be exalted with honour.
Whosoever he be that doth rebel against thy commandment, and will not hearken unto thy words in all that thou commandest him, he shall be put to death: only be strong and of a good courage.
Which removeth the mountains, and they know not: which overturneth them in his anger.

And he knoweth their prayers, that they were in behalf of their brethren. And he knoweth their faith, for in his name could they remove mountains; and in his name could they cause the earth to shake; and by the power of his word did they cause prisons to tumble to the earth; yea, even the fiery furnace could not harm them, neither wild beasts nor poisonous serpents, because of the power of his…
He putteth forth his hand upon the rock; he overturneth the mountains by the roots.
But now, because it is not so, he hath visited in his anger; yet he knoweth it not in great extremity:
And as they came down from the mountain, he charged them that they should tell no man what things they had seen, till the Son of man were risen from the dead.

And he said, Go forth, and stand upon the mount before the Lord. And, behold, the Lord passed by, and a great and strong wind rent the mountains, and brake in pieces the rocks before the Lord; but the Lord was not in the wind: and after the wind an earthquake; but the Lord was not in the earthquake:
Which shaketh the earth out of her place, and the pillars thereof tremble.
Therefore I will shake the heavens, and the earth shall remove out of her place, in the wrath of the Lord of hosts, and in the day of his fierce anger.
Therefore, I will shake the heavens, and the earth shall remove out of her place, in the wrath of the Lord of Hosts, and in the day of his fierce anger.
For after your testimony cometh the testimony of earthquakes, that shall cause groanings in the midst of her, and men shall fall upon the ground and shall not be able to stand.
She dwelleth and abideth on the rock, upon the crag of the rock, and the strong place.
Which commandeth the sun, and it riseth not; and sealeth up the stars.
The sun also ariseth, and the sun goeth down, and hasteth to his place where he arose.
And thus, according to his word the earth goeth back, and it appeareth unto man that the sun standeth still; yea, and behold, this is so; for surely it is the earth that moveth and not the sun.
The sun and the moon shall be darkened, and the stars shall withdraw their shining.
And behold, there shall a new star arise, such an one as ye never have beheld; and this also shall be a sign unto you.
Which alone spreadeth out the heavens, and treadeth upon the waves of the sea.
Behold, he spreadeth his light upon it, and covereth the bottom of the sea.
Let the heavens rejoice, and let the earth be glad; let the sea roar, and the fulness thereof.

¶ Thus saith God the Lord, he that created the heavens, and stretched them out; he that spread forth the earth, and that which cometh out of it; he that giveth breath unto the people upon it, and spirit to them that walk therein:
Which maketh Arcturus, Orion, and Pleiades, and the chambers of the south.
Canst thou bind the sweet influences of Pleiades, or loose the bands of Orion?

And  besides  the  stars  above  mentioned  the  band  of  the 
Pleiades  is  also  made  up  of  seven  stars,  the  rising  and  occulta- 
tion  of  which  are  the  causes  of  great  benefits  to  all  men. 
For  when  they  set,  the  furrows  are  ploughed  up  for  the  pur- 
pose of  sowing  ;  and  when  they  are  about  to  rise,  they  bring 
glad  tidings  of  harvest ;  and  after …

As the Pleiades rise, a beautiful reddish star of the first magnitude
rises beneath them. It is called Aldebaran, and it, as well as the
Pleiades, forms a part of the constellation of Taurus the bull. In
England we can see in winter below Aldebaran the whole of the
constellation of Orion, one of the finest of all the constellations,
both for the number of the bright stars it contains and for the…
Which doeth great things past finding out; yea, and wonders without number.
Which doeth great things and unsearchable; marvellous things without number:

10. And the time speedily cometh that great things are to be shown
    forth unto the children of men;

Among all the great signs and wonders oi the world, from the beginning till now, not one has left so lasting and incontrovertible u witness as truth. The wisdom of ages, the inventions of thousands, and the majesty of au thnriiy, combined with the pomp, circumstance, eclat and sycophancy of cozening millions, have passed in their time, like the shining meteor or trackless wind, into the region of…
Lo, he goeth by me, and I see him not: he passeth on also, but I perceive him not.
And he that taketh not his cross, and followeth after me, is not worthy of me.
He that passeth by, and meddleth with strife belonging not to him, is like one that taketh a dog by the ears.
He that is not with me is against me: and he that gathereth not with me scattereth.
Behold, I go forward, but he is not there; and backward, but I cannot perceive him:
And if he come to see me, he speaketh vanity: his heart gathereth iniquity to itself; when he goeth abroad, he telleth it.
Behold, he taketh away, who can hinder him? who will say unto him, What doest thou?
Thou prevailest for ever against him, and he passeth: thou changest his countenance, and sendest him away.

They bear him upon the shoulder, they carry him, and set him in his place, and he standeth; from his place shall he not remove: yea, one shall cry unto him, yet can he not answer, nor save him out of his trouble.
If he destroy him from his place, then it shall deny him, saying, I have not seen thee.

And he that will contend against the word of the Lord, let him be accursed; and he that shall deny these things, let him be accursed; for unto them will I show no greater things, saith Jesus Christ; for I am he who speaketh.
And spake unto him, saying, Tell us, by what authority doest thou these things? or who is he that gave thee this authority?
If God will not withdraw his anger, the proud helpers do stoop under him.

Do not get so angry that you cannot pray; do not allow yourselves to
become so angry that you cannot feed an enemy--even your worst enemy,
if an opportunity should {414} present itself. There is a wicked anger,
and there is a righteous anger. The Lord does not suffer wicked anger
to be in his heart; but there is anger in his bosom, and he will hold a
controversy with the nations, and will sift…
Who can tell if God will turn and repent, and turn away from his fierce anger, that we perish not?
Shall they escape by iniquity? in thine anger cast down the people, O God.
But he giveth more grace. Wherefore he saith, God resisteth the proud, but giveth grace unto the humble.
How much less shall I answer him, and choose out my words to reason with him?
For he is not a man, as I am, that I should answer him, and we should come together in judgment.
Should not the multitude of words be answered? and should a man full of talk be justified?
So shall I have wherewith to answer him that reproacheth me: for I trust in thy word.
Now he hath not directed his words against me: neither will I answer him with your speeches.
Therefore, he that lacketh wisdom, let him ask of me, and I will give him liberally and upbraid him not.
Whom, though I were righteous, yet would I not answer, but I would make supplication to my judge.
There the righteous might dispute with him; so should I be delivered for ever from my judge.
Judge me, O Lord my God, according to thy righteousness; and let them not rejoice over me.
As it is written, There is none righteous, no, not one:
Whom I would have retained with me, that in thy stead he might have ministered unto me in the bonds of the gospel:
I came not to call the righteous, but sinners to repentance.
If I had called, and he had answered me; yet would I not believe that he had hearkened unto my voice.
And Moses answered and said, But, behold, they will not believe me, nor hearken unto my voice: for they will say, The Lord hath not appeared unto thee.
How is it that ye have not hearkened unto the word of the Lord?
But verily God hath heard me; he hath attended to the voice of my prayer.
And when he looked behind him, he saw me, and called unto me. And I answered, Here am I.
Because he hath inclined his ear unto me, therefore will I call upon him as long as I live.
For he breaketh me with a tempest, and multiplieth my wounds without cause.
He healeth the broken in heart, and bindeth up their wounds.
He breaketh me with breach upon breach, he runneth upon me like a giant.
¶ But the Lord sent out a great wind into the sea, and there was a mighty tempest in the sea, so that the ship was like to be broken.
Behold, he findeth occasions against me, he counteth me for his enemy,
He will not suffer me to take my breath, but filleth me with bitterness.
What shall I say? he hath both spoken unto me, and himself hath done it: I shall go softly all my years in the bitterness of my soul.
He hath filled me with bitterness, he hath made me drunken with wormwood.
And also he hath need to repent, for I, the Lord, am not well pleased with him, for he seeketh to excel, and he is not sufficiently meek before me.
For thou writest bitter things against me, and makest me to possess the iniquities of my youth.
Therefore I will not refrain my mouth; I will speak in the anguish of my spirit; I will complain in the bitterness of my soul.
If I speak of strength, lo, he is strong: and if of judgment, who shall set me a time to plead?
Will he plead against me with his great power? No; but he would put strength in me.
And if any man among you be strong in the Spirit, let him take with him him that is weak, that he may be edified in all meekness, that he may become strong also.
The steps of his strength shall be straitened, and his own counsel shall cast him down.
With him is wisdom and strength, he hath counsel and understanding.
Or let him take hold of my strength, that he may make peace with me; and he shall make peace with me.
If I justify myself, mine own mouth shall condemn me: if I say, I am perfect, it shall also prove me perverse.
Thine own mouth condemneth thee, and not I: yea, thine own lips testify against thee.

Condemn me not because of mine imperfection, neither my father, because of his imperfection, neither them who have written before him; but rather give thanks unto God that he hath made manifest unto you our imperfections, that ye may learn to be more wise than we have been.
All the words of my mouth are in righteousness; there is nothing froward or perverse in them.
I will say unto God, Do not condemn me; shew me wherefore thou contendest with me.
My lips shall not speak wickedness, nor my tongue utter deceit.
Though I were perfect, yet would I not know my soul: I would despise my life.
Oh, thought I, that I could be banished and become extinct both soul and body, that I might not be brought to stand in the presence of my God, to be judged of my deeds.
Nevertheless, I would not suffer that ye should part until you were chastened for all your sins, that you might be one, that you might not perish in wickedness;
My soul is weary of my life; I will leave my complaint upon myself; I will speak in the bitterness of my soul.
So that my soul chooseth strangling, and death rather than my life.
Yea, it grieveth my soul and causeth me to shrink with shame before the presence of my Maker, that I must testify unto you concerning the wickedness of your hearts.
This is one thing, therefore I said it, He destroyeth the perfect and the wicked.
The righteousness of the perfect shall direct his way: but the wicked shall fall by his own wickedness.
The Lord preserveth all them that love him: but all the wicked will he destroy.

with him, and the wicked will be destroyed from off the face of the earth. I meant to be short this afternoon; but really, when I get to studying on these things, I forget myself, and oftentimes weary the patience of the people. God bless you! Amen.
The way of the wicked is an abomination unto the Lord: but he loveth him that followeth after righteousness.
Is not thy wickedness great? and thine iniquities infinite?
If the scourge slay suddenly, he will laugh at the trial of the innocent.
And they shall mock him, and shall scourge him, and shall spit upon him, and shall kill him: and the third day he shall rise again.
And now behold, we will detect this man, and he shall confess his fault and make known unto us the true murderer of this judge.
The revenger of blood himself shall slay the murderer: when he meeteth him, he shall slay him.
Or in enmity smite him with his hand, that he die: he that smote him shall surely be put to death; for he is a murderer: the revenger of blood shall slay the murderer, when he meeteth him.
And they shall scourge him, and put him to death: and the third day he shall rise again.
The earth is given into the hand of the wicked: he covereth the faces of the judges thereof; if not, where, and who is he?
That bringeth the princes to nothing; he maketh the judges of the earth as vanity.
The Lord is known by the judgment which he executeth: the wicked is snared in the work of his own hands. Higgaion. Selah.
The Lord lifteth up the meek: he casteth the wicked down to the ground.
I, the Lord, am angry with the wicked; I am holding my Spirit from the inhabitants of the earth.
Now my days are swifter than a post: they flee away, they see no good.
Wherefore have I seen them dismayed and turned away back? and their mighty ones are beaten down, and are fled apace, and look not back: for fear was round about, saith the Lord.

My people hath been lost sheep: their shepherds have caused them to go astray, they have turned them away on the mountains: they have gone from mountain to hill, they have forgotten their restingplace.
They abhor me, they flee far from me, and spare not to spit in my face.

Now they durst not turn to the right nor to the left lest they should be surrounded; neither would I turn to the right nor to the left lest they should overtake me, and we could not stand against them, but be slain, and they would make their escape; and thus we did flee all that day into the wilderness, even until it was dark.
But behold, ye are gone, and my sorrows cannot bring your return.
They are passed away as the swift ships: as the eagle that hasteth to the prey.
¶ And these are they which ye shall have in abomination among the fowls; they shall not be eaten, they are an abomination: the eagle, and the ossifrage, and the ospray,
They shall roar like young lions; yea, they shall roar, and lay hold of the prey, and shall carry away safe, and none shall deliver.
If I say, I will forget my complaint, I will leave off my heaviness, and comfort myself:
When I say, My bed shall comfort me, my couch shall ease my complaint;
I poured out my complaint before him; I shewed before him my trouble.
Attend unto me, and hear me: I mourn in my complaint, and make a noise;
I am afraid of all my sorrows, I know that thou wilt not hold me innocent.
My flesh trembleth for fear of thee; and I am afraid of thy judgments.
But there is forgiveness with thee, that thou mayest be feared.
And of whom hast thou been afraid or feared, that thou hast lied, and hast not remembered me, nor laid it to thy heart? have not I held my peace even of old, and thou fearest me not?
I am a companion of all them that fear thee, and of them that keep thy precepts.
Nevertheless, thou hast seen great sorrow, for thou hast rejected me many times because of pride and the cares of the world.
If I be wicked, why then labour I in vain?
Then I said, I have laboured in vain, I have spent my strength for nought, and in vain: yet surely my judgment is with the Lord, and my work with my God.
For he knoweth vain men: he seeth wickedness also; will he not then consider it?
Incline not my heart to any evil thing, to practise wicked works with men that work iniquity: and let me not eat of their dainties.
Who rejoice to do evil, and delight in the frowardness of the wicked;
Then I said, I have labored in vain, I have spent my strength for naught and in vain; surely my judgment is with the Lord, and my work with my God.
If I wash myself with snow water, and make my hands never so clean;
Purge me with hyssop, and I shall be clean: wash me, and I shall be whiter than snow.
¶ Then will I sprinkle clean water upon you, and ye shall be clean: from all your filthiness, and from all your idols, will I cleanse you.
And whomsoever he toucheth that hath the issue, and hath not rinsed his hands in water, he shall wash his clothes, and bathe himself in water, and be unclean until the even.
That he might sanctify and cleanse it with the washing of water by the word,
Yet shalt thou plunge me in the ditch, and mine own clothes shall abhor me.
And if thou deal thus with me, kill me, I pray thee, out of hand, if I have found favour in thy sight; and let me not see my wretchedness.
Thou shalt sell me meat for money, that I may eat; and give me water for money, that I may drink: only I will pass through on my feet;

And I will also give thee into their hand, and they shall throw down thine eminent place, and shall break down thy high places: they shall strip thee also of thy clothes, and shall take thy fair jewels, and leave thee naked and bare.
That which was torn of beasts I brought not unto thee; I bare the loss of it; of my hand didst thou require it, whether stolen by day, or stolen by night.
I will do these things unto thee, because thou hast gone a whoring after the heathen, and because thou art polluted with their idols.
For he is not a man, as I am, that I should answer him, and we should come together in judgment.
How much less shall I answer him, and choose out my words to reason with him?
If any of you lack wisdom, let him ask of God, that giveth to all men liberally, and upbraideth not; and it shall be given him.
Neither is there any daysman betwixt us, that might lay his hand upon us both.
That which hath been is named already, and it is known that it is man: neither may he contend with him that is mightier than he.
Lay down now, put me in a surety with thee; who is he that will strike hands with me?
Neither is worshipped with men’s hands, as though he needed any thing, seeing he giveth to all life, and breath, and all things;
Yea, before the day was I am he; and there is none that can deliver out of my hand: I will work, and who shall let it?
Let him take his rod away from me, and let not his fear terrify me:
Shall not his excellency make you afraid? and his dread fall upon you?
Therefore am I troubled at his presence: when I consider, I am afraid of him.
All ye that are about him, bemoan him; and all ye that know his name, say, How is the strong staff broken, and the beautiful rod!
What will ye? shall I come unto you with a rod, or in love, and in the spirit of meekness?
Then would I speak, and not fear him; but it is not so with me.
Then I said unto you, Dread not, neither be afraid of them.
Shall it be told him that I speak? if a man speak, surely he shall be swallowed up.
But he saith unto them, It is I; be not afraid.
